nativeRange.habitat: Slow- to moderate-flowing rivers and streams in lowland tropical forest; often found in shaded, well-vegetated stretches with soft, slightly acidic water over sandy or leaf-littered substrates

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back (Amazon sword, Vallisneria, Cryptocoryne)
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce surface jumping
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and refuge

diet.notes: Accepts most standard aquarium foods readily. Offer high-quality flake or micro-pellets as a staple, supplemented with small frozen or live foods (brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworm, micro-worms) for conditioning and colour enhancement. Vegetable-based flake and spirulina content is beneficial. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, insect larvae, plant material, and algae.

goodWith: dwarf cichlids (Pelvicachromis, Nanochromis) kept with caution
avoidWith: small fish or fry that may be eaten
species requiring hard alkaline water incompatible with preferred parameters
African Banded Barb (Enteromius fasciolatus fw-fish-242) — superficially similar but unrelated (Cyprinidae); different water and social needs

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Arnoldichthys spilopterus; shows characteristic lateral stripe and red-tinted eye. Check current URL availability at time of upload.

summary: The Niger Tetra (Arnoldichthys spilopterus) is a schooling West African fish from Nigeria and Cameroon, reaching up to 7.5 cm. It is recognized by a bold lateral stripe, iridescent flanks, and a distinctive red upper iris. Generally regarded as peaceful and easy to keep, it does best in groups of 8 or more and requires a secure lid, as it is an accomplished jumper.
careNotes:
The Niger Tetra (Arnoldichthys spilopterus) is one of the relatively few African characiforms available through the aquarium trade, found primarily through specialist importers and online retailers rather than general pet stores. It belongs to the family Alestidae — the same family as the Congo Tetra — rather than the South American Characidae, despite sharing the 'tetra' common name. It should not be confused with the African Banded Barb (Enteromius fasciolatus, fw-fish-242), a cyprinid of a completely different family that is occasionally sold under similar trade names.

Keep in schools of at least 8 individuals; smaller groups produce timid, stressed fish that lose colour and may hide. A tank of 100 L or more with a minimum 90 cm footprint is recommended to give the school adequate open swimming space. Soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15) mirrors the West African lowland river habitats of Nigeria and Cameroon. Temperature should be maintained in the 23–28 °C range.

A secure, tight-fitting lid is essential — the Niger Tetra is an accomplished jumper and escape risk is high. Floating plants help break the water surface and discourage jumping while also providing a more naturalistic light environment. The species adapts well to most prepared foods and presents no significant feeding challenge, making it a reasonable choice for anyone comfortable with basic aquarium maintenance. Water quality should be maintained with regular partial water changes; the species is sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes but tolerates a normal range of nitrate if changes are consistent.

The distinctive red iris spot (upper portion of the eye) is one of the easiest field marks for identification. Males tend to be slimmer and display more vivid colouration; females are noticeably deeper-bodied, especially when carrying eggs.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is possible but infrequently achieved in community tanks. A dedicated breeding tank of 60–80 L with very soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.8, gH 1–6) and subdued lighting is recommended. Condition a group of well-fed adults over several weeks with live and frozen foods. Spawning is egg-scattering; the adults will eat eggs readily, so a substrate of fine-leaved plants (Java moss, fine-mesh spawning mop) or a mesh bottom is advisable to separate eggs from parents. Remove adults after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 30–36 hours at 26–27 °C; fry become free-swimming after 3–4 days and should be fed infusoria or commercial fry foods initially, graduating to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp as they grow. Breeding difficulty is considered moderate due to the need for conditioned pairs, soft water, and fry management.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Arnoldichthys spilopterus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~7 cm), temperature 23–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–268 ppm (≈1–15 dGH), native range (Niger Delta and coastal rivers of Nigeria, also Cameroon), biotope, diet, breeding notes, and compatibility. Confirmed family Alestidae and order Characiformes.

additional.name: FishBase — Arnoldichthys spilopterus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (7 cm TL), temperature range, native range (Nigeria, Cameroon — coastal rivers), taxonomy (Alestidae, Characiformes), and diet. Confirmed monotypic genus Arnoldichthys. Native range data consistent with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: General tetra husbandry reference; confirmed omnivore diet approach, schooling requirements, ease of care, and food acceptance. No dedicated Niger Tetra page available; general tetra guidance applied where species-specific data were consistent across other sources.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Arnoldichthys spilopterus species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source; confirmed West African origin, escape risk, schooling behaviour requirements (groups of 8+), minimum tank size guidance, and compatibility notes. Noted the species is often confused with superficially similar African fish sold under overlapping trade names.

additional.name: Fishbase — Alestidae family overview
additional.notes: Reference for Alestidae family confirmation; used to verify the Niger Tetra is an African characiform distinct from the South American Characidae. Supports the notable taxonomic callout in the species record.

fish.breedingNotes: The Niger Tetra is an egg scatterer that is infrequently bred in community aquaria. Successful spawning requires a dedicated soft-water breeding tank with fine-leaved plant cover or a mesh bottom to protect eggs from being eaten by the adults, which should be removed promptly after spawning. Fry are initially fed infusoria or commercial fry food, graduating to baby brine shrimp as they grow.
